title: Map container user to a "real" user
body:
/kind feature

Hello,

i would like to map a container user to a "real" user. It would make the development process a lot easier. Im mounting local code directory into a container, then edit the code on the native side. It would be really nice, if the container user would be mapped to the native user. This would allow normal access on both sides, the container and the native side.

i have tried to join the irc channel, but i or the matrix.org-irc-bridge is banned on the #podman channel.

comments.body:
Hi @chpio,

thanks for opening the issue.

> i would like to map a container user to a "real" user. It would make the development process a lot easier. Im mounting local code directory into a container, then edit the code on the native side. It would be really nice, if the container user would be mapped to the native user. This would allow normal access on both sides, the container and the native side.

This should work already. If you are running on an selinux-enabled system you need to add the `Z` option to the volume/mount, e.g. 
```
$ podman run -v `pwd`:/test:Z alpine touch /test/foo
```

> i have tried to join the irc channel, but i or the matrix.org-irc-bridge is banned on the #podman channel.

I am using Matrix as well. The #podman channel requires users to be registered on Freenode. Can you check if your user is registered and retry?

I don't know if this is helping me, right now the `root` user is mapped to the executing user on the host, but i want also map another container user (in my case `www-data`/uid:33) to also my host user. I guess that's not possible?
I am sure @rhatdan or @giuseppe know some tricks?

You want to run a rootless container that treats your UID as your UID inside of the container.
Have you tried out toolbox?  It is a wrapper around podman that I think does what you want.

Rootless Podman defaults to mapping your UID to root inside of the container. But toolbox modifies podman's default behaviour to do mount the first UID of the /etc/subuid range to root inside of the container and then maps your UID into the container. 

Lets look at the default case of podman for a user test with UID=1000.
and /etc/subuild 
test:100000:65536

Podman sets up this mapping
         0       1000          1
         1     100000      65536

Toolbox tells podman to setup a different mapping

         0        100000       1000
         1000  1000           1
         1001  101001       64536

Hand edited, so I might have gotten this wrong.

podman run --security-opt label=disabled -v /home/test:/home/test --uidmap 0:100000:1000 --uidmap 1000:1000:1 --uidmap 1001:101001:64536 --workdir=${PWD} fedora sh
fedora-toolbox is doing what @rhatdan suggested.  Please reopen if this is still an issue

I don't think the --uidmap example that @rhatdan posted is accurate for rootless containers. There's some surprising behavior described in #6123 where you need to use `0` for the host UID instead of your real UID.

If you want to map a host UID to a container UID directly, you can use `--userns=keep-id`, but if you need to map the current host UID to a different container UID, you can use something like this: `--user $container_uid --uidmap $container_uid:0:1 --uidmap=0:1:1`.

(using Podman v3.0.1)

Docker solves this elegantly:

$cat /etc/subuid
dockremap:500000:1
dockremap:1:65535

is all that it takes.

In this way I can use my current user ID as the default user ID inside the docker container (and edit my host-based files without worries), while the root inside the container maps to a special user ID.

Why the strange complaint from podman that the ranges in /etc/subuid include the current user ID? It should be none of podman's concerns.

Same problem is raised here: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/5751#issue-595918773

LE: let alone one has to install a "toolbox" for it.

> $cat /etc/subuid
> dockremap:500000:1
> dockremap:1:65535

Looks like you would like a way to define `--userns=keep-id` by default?

I don't think depending on a non-default configuration in `/etc/subuid` is better.   By default, only the additional IDs are added to `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` so doing what you are showing above requires root privileges.

Moving this complexity to the `podman unshare` stage will make things more difficult to handle from Podman, e.g. how would `--userns=nomap` work in this case or `--userns=keep-id`?
